5. Representations and Symmetry

5.1. Steerable Filters and Spherical Harmonics

“Steerable” is a property of a representation: you can apply a continuous group action by a known transformation in representation space, without going back to the underlying continuous signal and resampling.

Definition 2. A steerable filter is a filter family such that the kernel at any orientation can be written as a linear combination of a fixed, finite set of basis kernels, much smaller than the dimension of all possible kernels.

In other words is a low dimensional subspace of the space of all kernels.

Example: Filter family is first directional derivative of a 2D Gaussian at any angle. Spanned by and derivatives. Second derivative is spanned by three basis elements:

Convolution vs cross correlation formula

The general recipe for building a steerable filter is to find a subspace of kernels that is invariant under the group representation. In particular:

  1. Find a linear operator such that …

    1. …it commutes with the group action. This means that its eigenspace is unchanged under the group action.
    2. …its eigenvectors are guaranteed to be a complete basis for the function space (it is self-adjoint).
  2. Compute its Eigendecomposition.
  3. The eigenfunctions then form a steerable basis for the group transformation!

Example 1 (Building a SO2 steerable filter family). Fourier modes form a complete orthonormal basis on the circle, and they are eigenfunctions of every rotation operator . Hence the span of any subset of modes is invariant under rotations.

Now, a 2D kernel can be written as which can be written as a sum of Fourier modes whose coefficients depend on :

(4)

( gets split into sine and cosine in 2D.)

To make it low dimensional, we pick a few radial basis functions for each value of : , making the basis count .

Example 2 (Building a SO3 steerable filter family). Fourier modes form an orthonormal eigenbasis on the circle4. Spherical harmonics do for a sphere. Both are eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator5, which is self-adjoint and commutes with rotations.

Hence the span of a subset of spherical harmonics yields an invariant

Generally need to include all or none of each since in the spherical harmonic basis, the representation looks like the Wigner-D matrices which is block diagonal in each (irreps).

[TODO] Derive Wigner-D

Applications of spherical harmonics:

  • Distill NeRF6 (PlenOctrees7)
  • Represent scene of Gaussian splatting: each 3D gaussian stores a set of spherical harmonic coefficients for directional color.

Alternative local bases for storing functions on the sphere are not steerable.

  • Geodesic sphere / Subdivision of icosahedron.
  • Cube map: direction face on cube and 2D coordinates on that face

Cube maps are preferred when high resolution representation of angular variation is wanted. Otherwise people use spherical harmonics. Cost of spectral basis is that you need to sum all the basis functions just to evaluate at one direction. [TODO] Cube map is only steerable with respect to the cube symmetry subgroup of SO3.

5.2. Geometric SIgnal Processing

  • Want to extend tools of images to mesh or point cloud. With images, the domain is a coordinate and the range is RGB/intensity. But a point cloud is usually just coordinates.
  • We extend the Laplacian for discrete meshes via cotangent Laplacian.
  • From eigenanalysis, shape of drum does not uniquely determine frequencies (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_the_shape_of_a_drum)
  • Useful review: Computing and Processing Correspondences with Functional Maps

  • Laplace Beltrami focuses on intrinsic geometry (observable by measurements purely on the 2D surface). Extrinsic geometry refers to the 3D ambient space.

    • Plane = cylinder in intrinsic geometry but not intrinsic

5.3. Geometric Deep Learning

So far convolution is not well defined on point sets or triangle mesh.

Some architectures:

  • PointNet: Per-point MLP Permutation invariant aggregation (max pooling8) Global processing. For segmentation the global feature is concatenated back to per-point features and further processed per-point. Qi et al. prove that any continuous set function can be approximated in this form under mild conditions.
  • PointNet++: Hierarchical version of PointSet (kinda like CNN): (1) pick centroids, (2) group nearby points around each centroid via kNN, (3) run a local PointNet inside each group to get a local feature, and (4) treat those local features as a new (smaller) point set for the next level.
  • Dynamic Graph CNN: Predates attention, uses k-nearest neighbors and some sort of GNN/aggregation step.
  • Voxelize everything to get back to (sparse) square lattice: Sparse voxel CNN, MinkovskiNet, VoxelNet

5.4. Equivariant Deep Learning

CNNs are not truely shift invariance because the downsamping is done by brute force without anti-aliasing. Making Convolutional Networks Shift-Invariant Again adds a blur before pooling that helps when data is limited but otherwise doesn’t help that much.

Example 3 (Designing equivariant CNN layers). Two common consrtuctinos:

  1. Take normal CNN layer and apply Reynold’s projection
  2. Group convolution: add a new feature dimension for the group where we apply a rotated filter. Group pooling causes invariance, so wait till the end if you want equivariance. This is not just test-time augmentation since intermediate features mix in an equivariant way ([TODO] wait for sym4ml).
  3. Parameterizing the filters by restricting kernel to solution space of equivariance constraint: . Parmeterize it as a linear combination of a basis for that solution basis which can be constructed eiither nalytically from steerable/irrep bases or numerical by solving the equation. [TODO] is this allowed by Schur’s lemma? Wait for sym4ml.

Run transcript through chatgpt and check for mixed

  1. 1Multiple observations, e.g. our eyes yield two observations by different camera pose
  2. 2Such lines are define as orthogonal to the plane normal: Substitute
  3. 3Upsample by inserting zeros (dilation) and then convolving with a filter that grabs stuff around the central pixel.
  4. 4Consider the Hermitian operator or
  5. 5Generalizes from Euclidean space to a curved Riemannian manifold. Eigenfunctions play the role of Fourier modes on .
  6. 6A NeRF (Neural Radiance Field) represents a scene as a continuous function that maps a 3D point and viewing direction to a density and emitted color , learned from posed images (often with positional encoding to capture high frequencies)

  7. 7An octree is used because NeRF scenes are sparse in 3D: most of the volume is empty, and the interesting geometry occupies a small subset of space. An octree adaptively refines only where the field varies (near surfaces, fine detail) and keeps large cells in empty/smooth regions, which reduces memory versus a dense voxel grid and accelerates rendering via empty-space skipping and fewer samples per ray.

    Yes: in PlenOctrees the octree is an explicit, discretized approximation of a trained NeRF. You evaluate the NeRF at spatial locations covered by the octree, store σ(x) and a compact directional color model per cell (e.g., SH coefficients), and then render from this stored structure instead of querying the NeRF network.

  8. 8This makes the representation beavhe like a learned “presence” detector over points per feature dimension (more like “is there a point that triggers feature j?” than “how many / how much?”).
